title: Marketing Management
summary: Marketing is the social process by which individuals and groups obtain what they need through creating, offering, and exchanging products of value.
Marketing is about identifying customer needs and satisfying them profitably. Anything of value can be marketed — products, services, persons, places.
Product: Anything offered to satisfy needs. Includes branding, labelling, and packaging.
Branding: Giving a distinct identity (name, symbol, design). Labelling: Information on the product. Packaging: Primary, Secondary, Transportation packaging.
Price: Amount charged. Factors: Cost, Demand, Competition, Objectives, Government regulation.
Place (Distribution): Getting products from producer to consumer. Channels: Direct, One-level (1 intermediary), Two-level (2 intermediaries), Three-level.
Promotion: Informing and persuading customers. Elements: Advertising, Personal Selling, Sales Promotion, Public Relations.
This chapter carries 11 marks — the highest weightage in the entire syllabus.

teacher_tip.title: AKSpected Shortcut
teacher_tip.text: Remember: 'Marketing ≠ Selling'. Marketing starts before production (identifying needs); Selling starts after production (pushing existing products). If the question says 'free sample/contest/discount' → Sales Promotion. 'Paid, non-personal message through media' → Advertising. 'Face-to-face interaction with customer' → Personal Selling. 'Building company image, press conferences' → Public Relations.

definition.text: A social process by which individuals and groups obtain what they need and want through creating, offering, and freely exchanging products and services of value with others.

sections.title: Marketing Mix — 4Ps
sections.content: Marketing Mix is the set of controllable variables that a firm blends to produce the response it wants from the target market.
points: Product: What you sell — goods, services, ideas.
Price: What you charge for it.
Place: Where and how you make it available.
Promotion: How you communicate about it.

sections.title: Product — Branding, Labelling, Packaging
points: Branding: Giving a distinct name, symbol, or design to identify and differentiate a product. Example: Nike swoosh, Apple logo.
• Brand Name: The spoken part — 'Nike', 'Amul'.
• Brand Mark: The visual symbol — Nike's swoosh.
• Trademark: Legal protection of the brand (™ or ®).
Labelling: All written information on the product or package. Includes: Brand name, Ingredients, Manufacturing/Expiry date, MRP, Nutritional info, Statutory warnings, Barcodes.
Packaging — 3 Levels:
• Primary: The immediate container (toothpaste tube).
• Secondary: Additional layer of protection (cardboard box around the tube).
• Transportation: For shipping and storage (cartons of boxes).

sections.title: Price — Factors Determining Price
points: 1. Cost: Price must cover cost of production, distribution, and selling + profit margin.
2. Demand: Higher demand can support higher prices. Price elasticity matters.
3. Competition: Competitor pricing influences what you can charge.
4. Marketing Objectives: Penetration pricing (low price for market share) vs Skimming (high price for premium positioning).
5. Government Regulation: Price controls, MRP regulations, taxation.

sections.title: Place — Channels of Distribution
points: Direct Channel: Producer → Consumer. Example: Dell selling laptops online.
One-Level: Producer → Retailer → Consumer. Example: Garments to retail stores.
Two-Level: Producer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer. Most common for consumer goods.
Three-Level: Producer → Agent → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer. Used when market is geographically spread.
Components of Physical Distribution: Order processing, Transportation, Warehousing, Inventory management.

sections.title: Promotion — Promotion Mix
points: 1. Advertising: Paid, non-personal communication through mass media (TV, newspaper, online). Reaches large audience. Merits: wide reach, builds brand. Limitations: impersonal, expensive, feedback hard to measure.

2. Personal Selling: Face-to-face interaction between seller and buyer. Most effective for high-value, complex products. Merits: immediate feedback, builds relationships. Limitations: expensive per contact, limited reach.
3. Sales Promotion: Short-term incentives to boost sales. Examples: Free samples, Discounts, Contests, Lucky draws, Cashbacks, Buy-one-get-one. Effective for new product launches.
4. Public Relations: Building goodwill and positive image. Tools: Press conferences, Sponsorships, Special events, Annual reports, Community programmes. Not paid advertising — earned media.

tables.title: Marketing vs Selling
tables.tip: Classic board question. Know all differences.

tables.title: Advertising vs Personal Selling
rows: High total cost, low per person
Low total cost, high per contact

questions.text: Free samples and contests are examples of:
questions.answer: Sales Promotion — short-term incentives to boost sales.

questions.text: Explain any three elements of the promotion mix.
questions.answer:
(i) Advertising: Paid, non-personal communication through mass media to reach a large audience.
(ii) Personal Selling: Face-to-face interaction between seller and buyer for persuasion and feedback.
(iii) Sales Promotion: Short-term incentives like discounts, free samples, and contests to boost immediate sales.

questions.text: Explain Branding. What are its components?
questions.answer: Branding is the process of giving a distinct name, symbol, or design to identify and differentiate a product from competitors. Components: (i) Brand Name — the spoken part (Nike). (ii) Brand Mark — the visual symbol (swoosh). (iii) Trademark — legal protection under law (™ or ®).

questions.text:
A company launches a new health drink. It offers free samples at malls, runs TV advertisements, and sends sales representatives to meet gym owners. The product is priced competitively and distributed through wholesale and retail channels.

Identify the elements of marketing mix used.
questions.answer:
1. Product: New health drink — the actual offering.
2. Price: 'Priced competitively' — pricing strategy considering competition.
3. Place: 'Wholesale and retail channels' — Two-level distribution (Producer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer).
4. Promotion: Three elements used — Sales Promotion (free samples), Advertising (TV ads), Personal Selling (sales reps meeting gym owners).

pyqs.text: Explain any four factors affecting the price of a product.
pyqs.answer:
(1) Cost: Price must cover production, distribution, and selling costs plus profit.
(2) Demand: Higher demand supports higher prices; elastic demand limits pricing.
(3) Competition: Competitor prices influence what can be charged.
(4) Government Regulation: MRP regulations, tax policies, and price controls.

related_chapters.title: Business Environment
related_chapters.reason: Social and economic environment affects marketing

related_chapters.title: Consumer Protection
related_chapters.reason: Consumer rights relate to marketing practices
